articles.title: Graves Dug Up to Make Way for Trump’s Golf Course
articles.description: Julia Demaree Nikhinson / via REUTERS Vietnam is demolishing graves and moving its dead to make way for a Trump Organization golf course. The Financial Times reported that a gravesite in the Chau Ninh commune of the Hung Yen province has already begun to be demolished so bodies can be exhumed ahead of the construction of the $1.5 billion leisure development. Trump’s son Eric has called the new resort “the envy of all of Asia and of the entire world,” but residents in the agricultural community in northern Vietnam are now leaving large “X” marks painted on the graves of their loved ones to show they have already removed their remains. Read more at The Daily Beast.

articles.title: Trump administration deported Florida Keys man with TPS to Haiti. He’s back in the U.S.
articles.description: Aerial view of houses destroyed by armed gangs in 2024 in the Solino neighborhood of Port-au-Prince, Haiti, on March 3, 2026. Haiti's presidential transitional council, which has run the impoverished Caribbean nation for nearly two years, on February 7, 2026, handed power to US-backed Prime Minister Alix Didier Fils-Aime, after failing to rein in rampant gang violence. Rubio said in February he was upbeat about progress in setting up a new UN-blessed force to suppress Haiti's powerful gangs and voiced hope that the country will finally hold elections this year for the first time in a decade. (Photo by Clarens SIFFROY / AFP via Getty Images)

articles.title: DILG mulls over reward for arrest of fugitive Bato dela Rosa
articles.description: MANILA, Philippines — Interior Chief Jonvic Remulla said Friday that his department was considering a reward for the arrest of fugitive Sen. Ronald “Bato” dela Rosa. The International Criminal Court (ICC) issued a warrant of arrest against Dela Rosa in connection with his alleged participation in former President Rodrigo Duterte’s bloody war on drugs. As the search for Bato dela Rosa enters its second week, asked whether the government was considering a reward for his capture and arrest, Remulla told reporters in Camp Karingal in Quezon City, “That’s possible. We are considering that.” “But, apparently, it is not effective yet. [...]... Keep on reading: DILG mulls over reward for arrest of fugitive Bato dela Rosa

articles.title: One dead in Brighton Park fire
articles.description:
One person died after a fire in Brighton Park Thursday night, according to Chicago fire officials.

Firefighters responded to a call of a rubbish fire at 5040 S. Campbell Avenue at 6:41 p.m., said Chicago Fire Chief Juan Hernandez. A man, whose age wasn’t known, was found on the first floor of the building and taken to Christ Hospital where he later died.

Due to the thick smoke and heavy fire, firefighters initially had to pull back, Hernandez said. As the fire spread and damaged the homes on either side of the initial building, more than 150 firefighters were called to the scene to assist.

A police officer had tried to knock on the door when the fire was first reported, and before firefighters could arrive, but was turned away until the smoke became heavier, Hernandez said.

The cause of the fire is under investigation.

articles.title: 2 drug suspects nabbed in Quezon; P2.1-million shabu seized
articles.description: LUCENA CITY — Police arrested two alleged high-value drug suspects and seized suspected “shabu” (crystal meth) worth up to P2.1 million during a buy-bust operation in Tiaong, Quezon, on Thursday. Colonel Romulo Albacea, director of the Quezon Police Provincial Office (QPPO), said anti-illegal drug operatives arrested the suspects, identified only by their aliases “Randy,” 49, and “Gary,” 53, in Barangay Lalig at around 6:25 a.m. The suspects allegedly sold P1,000 worth of shabu to an undercover police operative during the operation. articles.title: Cities are taking a surprising step to avoid feeding info to Trump's ICE: report
articles.description: Cities contracting with Flock Safety to run networks of license plate reader cameras are growing increasingly alarmed about the company's involvement with the Trump administration's mass deportation schemes, to the point that some are now covering their own cameras in trash bags, a new report revealed. According to 404 Media, one city bagging up its cameras is Dayton, Ohio, which followed "months of resident outrage, a scandal in which the city was sharing Flock camera data for immigration enforcement, apparently by accident, and a $30,000 audit into how the cameras are being used." They had to bag the cameras rather than take them down outright because "police there are unsure whether the cameras are still active, and the city also doesn’t seem to know whether it is allowed to take the cameras down." This follows a similar move by the city of Evanston, Illinois, which, following media reports that "data from the cameras was making its way to Immigration and Customs Enforcement through Flock’s national camera network," bagged up its cameras until Flock could uninstall them, said the report. These moves, 404 Media continued, show cities "feel that they do not have the ability to unilaterally decide when to stop using Flock surveillance cameras" under the terms of the contracts they signed. The American Civil Liberties Union has sounded the alarm on Flock, which they claim is building up the infrastructure for a "mass surveillance state" with its network of license plate readers that were sold to cities as a tool for public safety. Meanwhile, immigration enforcement is not the only politicized use of the Flock camera network. Last year, it was reported that a police officer in Johnson County, Texas, used a national Flock plate database to search for a woman who had an abortion .
